CS-13LOWSearch Findability
Product Card Alt Text Is Descriptive and Follows [Product Name] Template
Mobile product card alt text is descriptive and follows a clean `[Brand] [Product Name] [Fitment]` template — informative, fitment-specific, under 80 characters, no keyword stuffing. Indexable by Google Images and Lens.
What changes: No change needed. Maintain this pattern across the catalog.

Element

`img` at e22 (alt `VelourTex Fitted Carpet Floor Mats for the Ford Focus RS / ST (Set of 4)`)

Observation

All four featured collection product card images (e22–e25) carry descriptive alt text following the `[Brand] [Product Name] [Fitment]` template. Each alt text is informative, fitment-specific, and under 80 characters. No keyword stuffing detected.

Recommendation

No action required for the featured product card images. Maintain this alt text pattern for all product images site-wide.

Why this matters

Correctly described product images are indexed for Google Images and eligible for Google Lens visual search.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · baton.page_head.schema_jsonld
    The OnlineStore @type block contains sameAs: ['', 'https://www.facebook.com/awdmods', '', 'https://www.instagram.com/awdmods', '', '', '', 'https://www.youtube.com/@AWDMods', '', '', '', ''] — 9 of 12 entries are empty strings.

Source

Image SEO Alt Text
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
ET-01HIGHEthics
Privacy Policy Links to Staging Domain, Not Canonical Store
The footer Privacy Policy link points to a Shopify staging subdomain (`e1520g-k3.myshopify.com`) instead of awdmods.com. Every other footer link uses the right domain — only the Privacy Policy is misrouted. This is a fragile compliance posture: if the staging domain is ever retired or restricted, visitors hit a 404 at the exact moment they're trying to exercise privacy rights, creating an active GDPR Article 13 and CCPA notice violation.
What changes: In Shopify admin, navigate to Online Store > Navigation > footer menu, find the Privacy Policy link, and replace its destination URL with `/policies/privacy-policy` (relative) or `https://www.awdmods.com/policies/privacy-policy` (absolute). Two-minute fix.

Element

`contentinfo` at e30 (footer Information accordion; href `https://e1520g-k3.myshopify.com/policies/privacy-policy`)

Observation

The footer Privacy Policy link on both desktop and mobile points to `https://e1520g-k3.myshopify.com/policies/privacy-policy` — a `myshopify.com` staging subdomain — instead of a canonical first-party URL on `awdmods.com`. Every other footer link (About Us, Terms and Conditions, CARB Policy, Your Privacy Choices) correctly uses relative paths or the canonical domain. Only the Privacy Policy routes to the staging domain. The pattern is legally adjacent: GDPR Article 13 requires the controller to provide information `at the time personal data are obtained` via a clearly attributable notice; a privacy policy URL under a third-party domain (myshopify.com) rather than the merchant's own domain creates ambiguity about which entity controls the data and which policy governs the transaction. CCPA notice requirements similarly depend on visitors being able to identify the disclosing entity from the URL.

Recommendation

Update the Privacy Policy footer link to point to the canonical first-party URL. On Shopify, the standard path is `/policies/privacy-policy` (relative) or `https://www.awdmods.com/policies/privacy-policy` (absolute). In Shopify admin, navigate to Online Store > Navigation > footer menu, locate the Privacy Policy link, and replace the destination URL. Confirm the `Your Privacy Choices` page also links to the canonical policy rather than the staging domain.

Why this matters

A staging-domain privacy policy URL puts the site in a fragile compliance position. For any visitor from a GDPR-covered country (EU, UK, EEA) or California, the privacy policy is a legally required disclosure. If the staging domain is retired or access-restricted, visitors encounter a broken link at the exact moment they are trying to exercise their privacy rights — producing an active GDPR Art 13 and CCPA notice violation. The fix takes under two minutes in Shopify admin.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e19
    Desktop baton timers field absent. Full DOM scan found no countdown timer elements, 'sale ends', 'limited time', 'only X left', or 'people viewing' strings. Hero and featured collection sections contain no urgency-framing mechanisms.

Source

Research reference
BRONZE
ET-02LOWEthics
No Phantom Social Proof — Reviews Are Truthful With Explicit Count

Element

(absent)

Observation

Review ratings on the featured collection cards display '5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews' — a truthful disclosure that names the exact count. No phantom social proof pattern (filled stars with zero reviews, fabricated aggregate badges, undisclosed insider reviews) was detected on either device. No schema-level aggregateRating markup was found in the page_head JSON-LD that would conflict with on-page review counts.

Recommendation

Maintain explicit review count disclosure. As products accumulate more reviews, the star + count pattern will scale correctly without creating regulatory exposure.

Why this matters

FTC Fake Reviews Rule (16 CFR Part 465) prohibits misrepresenting review counts and fabricating social proof. Truthful disclosure of review counts eliminates this exposure class.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e21
    Desktop baton e21 text_content='5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews'. Review count is explicitly disclosed as (2). No filled star ratings displayed for products with zero reviews. No 'Rated and Reviewed' aggregate badges without underlying review data. The aggregate rating displayed reflects exactly 2 verified reviews.
  • DOM · e26
    Mobile baton e26 accessible_name='5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews' — same truthful disclosure confirmed on mobile.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-03LOWEthics
AI-Generated Images Are Decorative — FTC Materiality Test Passed

Element

(absent)

Observation

Seven AI-generated images (5x Gemini_Generated_Image_* filenames and 2x ChatGPT_Image_* filenames) appear on the page. Their uses are: (1) hero section atmospheric background (car in city scene, alt empty), (2) category tile card backgrounds for five navigation tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics), and (3) newsletter section background (two cars, atmospheric). All actual product listings in the Featured Collection section use real product photography. The AI images do not depict, imply performance specifications, warranty claims, or product capabilities for any specific SKU. Under the FTC Section 5 materiality test and ethics-gate.md Part 5.3 guidance, the materiality threshold is not met: a reasonable consumer would not make a different purchasing decision about a specific product based on the hero banner or category card atmospheric background being AI-generated vs. photographed. No BLOCK is warranted.

Recommendation

No compliance action required. As an optional best practice (not a legal requirement at this time), consider adding a brief site-level disclosure such as 'Some site imagery is AI-generated' in the footer. Major platforms (Amazon, Shopify, Google Shopping) are expected to formalize AI-image disclosure requirements for product listings by 2026-2027 — monitor for updates. Ensure that if AI-generated images are ever used in product-specific contexts (e.g., showing a specific exhaust system installed on a vehicle when no such installation photo exists), disclosure is added at that point.

Why this matters

FTC Operation AI Comply (Sep 2024) targeted deceptive AI capability claims and fake reviews — it did not pursue decorative atmospheric imagery. EU AI Act Article 50 deployer obligations do not reach decorative website backgrounds. The current use pattern is compliant. Continued vigilance is warranted as AI-image disclosure requirements are likely to evolve.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Hero banner background: ChatGPT_Image_Jan_5_2026_12_36_57_PM.png. Renders as atmospheric backdrop (Subaru WRX in city-night scene). alt='' (empty, treated as decorative). No product specifications, capabilities, or availability claims embedded in the image.
  • Visual · section-2.jpg @ y=513 (desktop)
    Category tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) each use an AI-generated background image (Gemini and ChatGPT filenames confirmed). Images depict automotive parts/interiors as atmospheric tile backgrounds — not product representations for any specific SKU being sold. Actual featured collection products (floor mats, decal kits, intakes, exhausts) use real product photography.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-04LOWEthics
Free Shipping Threshold Disclosed — No Drip Pricing Violation

Element

(absent)

Observation

The site displays a persistent announcement bar ('FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+') visible above the fold on both desktop and mobile at page load. CA SB-478 explicitly excludes carrier-calculated shipping from mandatory-fee disclosure requirements. The FTC Junk Fees Rule (16 CFR Part 464) is narrowly scoped to live-event ticketing and short-term lodging and does not apply here. No mandatory product-seller-imposed fees (processing fees, service charges, convenience fees) were identified in the homepage DOM. The displayed price format ('From $135.99', '$49.00', '$405.48') is consistent with pre-mandatory-fee pricing. Drip pricing BLOCK conditions are not met.

Recommendation

Maintain the above-fold free shipping threshold disclosure. If any mandatory non-shipping, non-government fees are introduced at checkout (e.g., a processing fee applied to all orders), these must be disclosed on the product page — not revealed for the first time at checkout.

Why this matters

Hidden fees account for 39-48% of cart abandonment. The current transparent disclosure both satisfies legal requirements and reduces abandonment. The FTC Junk Fees Rule and CA SB-478 mandatory-fee BLOCK conditions are clear.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Mobile announcement bar reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+'. Same threshold disclosure above fold.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    Announcement bar at top of page reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+ — Contiguous US only SHOP NOW'. Free shipping threshold is disclosed above the fold on the homepage before any user action.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-05LOWEthics
No Cookie Consent Banner Required — US-Only Targeting Confirmed

Element

(absent)

Observation

The site presents no cookie consent banner on either device capture. This is appropriate for a US-market-only store: (1) hreflang[] is empty — no EU/UK language/region targeting signals; (2) the announcement bar specifies 'Contiguous US only' shipping; (3) CCPA-specific 'Your Privacy Choices' link is present, indicating the operator's compliance posture is US-law-oriented. EU ePrivacy Art 5(3) and GDPR cookie consent requirements apply to sites with EU visitors — absent any EU traffic targeting, no cookie consent banner is required. Note: if the operator begins running EU-targeted advertising or adds EU hreflang tags, a compliant cookie consent banner must be implemented before EU traffic arrives.

Recommendation

Maintain the CCPA 'Your Privacy Choices' opt-out link. If EU or UK traffic is ever targeted (via paid advertising, hreflang, or EU fulfillment), implement a compliant cookie consent banner before enabling EU-targeted campaigns — prior-consent requirements under ePrivacy Art 5(3) apply from the moment EU visitors land on the page.

Why this matters

ePrivacy Art 5(3) and GDPR cookie consent requirements have produced the largest cookie-enforcement fines in EU regulatory history (CNIL fined Google €150M and Facebook €60M for consent-interface violations in 2022). The current US-only posture avoids this exposure. Expansion to EU markets without a compliant consent banner would immediately create BLOCK-class exposure.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e18
    page_head hreflang[] is empty (no EU/UK hreflang tags). Desktop baton overlays_detected[] is empty — no cookie consent banner detected at acquisition. Footer copy includes 'Your Privacy Choices' with CCPA icon and '/pages/data-sharing-opt-out' link — CCPA-specific disclosure confirms US market targeting. Cart drawer shipping calculator defaults to US; announcement bar states 'Contiguous US only'.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
ET-06LOWEthics
No Pre-Checked Boxes or Subscription Dark Patterns Detected

Element

(absent)

Observation

The newsletter signup (desktop e35, mobile e29/e33) requires explicit user action — entering an email address and clicking Subscribe. No pre-checked consent boxes, no confirm-shaming opt-out language, no automatic enrollment into paid subscription detected. No recurring billing subscription product is presented on the homepage. ROSCA conditions (automatic negative-option enrollment before billing info collection) are not present on this page.

Recommendation

Maintain explicit opt-in form for newsletter. If transactional SMS or push notifications are added, ensure CAN-SPAM/TCPA-compliant explicit written consent is collected before first message.

Why this matters

Pre-checked subscription boxes and confirm-shaming language are BLOCK-class violations under FTC Act Section 5 and EU DSA Art 25 (for in-scope platforms). Clean pass here eliminates ROSCA/CAN-SPAM exposure from the newsletter and checkout flow.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e35
    Newsletter section (e35) is a standard opt-in form with email input and subscribe button. No pre-checked checkbox, no confirm-shaming language, no hidden subscription enrollment. The form type is 'customer contact[email]' — standard Shopify newsletter signup with explicit affirmative action required (user must type email and click Subscribe). No ROSCA, CAN-SPAM, or dark-pattern BLOCK conditions are present.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PU-01MEDIUMPerformance & UX
Hamburger-Only Navigation Hides Primary Destinations
On mobile, the entire site navigation lives behind the hamburger icon — `Shop by Category`, `Shop by Vehicle`, Contact, My Account are all invisible until a visitor taps a small icon in the top-left. NNGroup research consistently shows hidden navigation costs 25–50% in engagement on essential functions, and AWDMods' two primary discovery modes are exactly the ones being hidden.
What changes: Add a persistent bottom navigation bar with 4–5 destinations (Home, Search, Shop by Vehicle, Cart, Account). Reserve the hamburger for the deeper category tree.

Element

`button` at e3 (hamburger toggle, top-left of sticky header)

Observation

The entire site navigation — Shop by Category, Shop by Vehicle, Contact, and My Account — lives behind a hamburger menu icon in the top-left corner. No persistent bottom navigation bar is present. On a 390px mobile viewport, the primary discovery paths (category browsing, vehicle-specific shopping) are invisible until the user taps a small icon. NNGroup research confirms hidden navigation consistently underperforms on discoverability; switching to visible bottom navigation increases engagement by 25–50% and reduces task completion time by 22%.

Recommendation

Implement a persistent bottom navigation bar with 4–5 destinations: Home, Search, Shop by Category (or Browse), Cart, Account. Reserve the hamburger for the full secondary category tree. The bottom bar should remain visible during scroll and display a badge count on Cart.

Why this matters

70% of users prefer bottom navigation over hamburger menus for essential functions. For a specialty parts store where vehicle compatibility is the primary filter, hiding `Shop by Vehicle` behind a hamburger creates an extra tap on every mobile session before a visitor can start shopping.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM + Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0
    Hero product image renders above the fold filling the full 390px viewport width. The page head contains preload declarations for Teko font and FontAwesome but no <link rel="preload" as="image"> for the hero product image. The img element has no fetchpriority attribute and no explicit width/height attributes — confirmed in header_html and page_head source.
  • DOM · e4

Source

Mobile Conversion
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PU-02HIGHPerformance & UX
No Sticky Add-to-Cart Bar After Scrolling Past CTA
The desktop AWDMods logo loads eagerly (good) but doesn't have the `fetchpriority='high'` hint that tells the browser to prioritize this image alongside critical CSS. The page head also has no image preload at all. fetchpriority is now supported by all major browsers and costs nothing to add.
What changes: Add `fetchpriority='high'` to the logo `<img>` element. If the hero gets converted to an `<img>`, give that one fetchpriority and drop the logo to default.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The Add to Cart button on this page exists only as a single inline element at roughly scroll position 860px CSS. After reading the Polaris Slingshot fitment guide (which spans 12 model years across sections 2 and 3) or the product description in section 3, a mobile visitor who decides to purchase must scroll back up past all that content to reach the button. There is no sticky bottom bar, no floating CTA, and no persistent purchase mechanism anywhere below the inline ATC position. This forces a disruptive reverse-scroll at the moment of highest purchase intent.

Recommendation

Add a sticky bottom bar that becomes visible once the user scrolls past the inline Add to Cart button. The bar should contain the product price ($69.95) and an Add to Cart button sized at minimum 60px height. If the product has no variant selectors that require selection before adding, the sticky bar can fire the ATC action directly. Ensure the bar does not obscure the bottom-navigation area on iOS Safari (add safe-area-inset-bottom padding). This is a component-level addition that applies only to the PDP template.

Why this matters

Mobile cart abandonment runs 8-10 percentage points higher than desktop. On a page where the fitment guide alone spans 12 model-year rows — content mobile shoppers actively read to confirm compatibility — the absence of a persistent CTA means the final scroll-back friction arrives exactly when intent peaks. Every pixel a buyer must scroll backward to complete a decided purchase is a moment where the session can be interrupted and lost.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=700 (mobile)
    The Add to Cart button appears inline at approximately scroll_y 860 CSS px. Once the user scrolls past this point into the fitment guide or product description, no persistent CTA is visible. Section 3 (scroll_y 2360) and sections 4-5 show no sticky bottom bar in any screenshot.
  • Visual · section-3-mobile.jpg @ y=1400 (mobile)
    Description and features section — no Add to Cart visible anywhere in the viewport. A mobile user reading the fitment guide or product description must scroll back up to purchase.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PU-03HIGHPerformance & UX
Desktop Header Renders Uncollapsed at 390px Viewport Width
Every image on the page is served as a PNG or JPEG — none are negotiated to AVIF or WebP, even though those formats are now supported by ~95% of browsers and reduce file size 25–50%. The DOM contains zero `<picture>` elements anywhere. Shopify supports AVIF/WebP natively; the theme just isn't using it. This is wasted bandwidth on every page load, slowing LCP and conversion.
What changes: Switch to Shopify's `image_url` Liquid filter with `format: 'webp'` (or `image_tag` for automatic format negotiation). Wrap product images in `<picture>` elements with AVIF and WebP sources before the JPEG/PNG fallback.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The header HTML contains a full desktop three-column Bootstrap 3 layout: a 4-column logo div, a 5-column div containing the phone number, search bar, and 'Call Us' label, and a 3-column div with Login/Sign Up links and the cart dropdown. The Bootstrap sm-breakpoint grid does not collapse at 390px (sm triggers at 768px in Bootstrap 3 default config). In the section-1-mobile.jpg screenshot, the rendered result is a compressed bar where the desktop header elements are squashed into the mobile viewport — the phone number, search input, and navigation links are visible but crowded. This is a desktop-first layout served to mobile without proper mobile breakpoint handling. The available header real estate on a 390px screen should be reserved for the logo and the two primary mobile actions (hamburger, cart) — not for Call Us phone numbers and login links that mobile users do not tap.

Recommendation

Apply CSS media query rules to hide the `#cs_top` call-us/login div and the `#search` bar within the desktop header at viewports below 768px. These functions should be accessible from within the hamburger drawer on mobile. The visible mobile header should contain only: logo (centered), hamburger icon (left), search icon (right), cart icon (right). This reduces header height from its current compressed state, frees approximately 40-60px of above-fold viewport height for product content, and eliminates the cognitive noise of crushed desktop UI elements at the top of every mobile page.

Why this matters

The header is the first thing a mobile visitor sees. A desktop layout compressed into a 390px viewport signals a site that is not optimized for mobile — which activates the speed-trust association documented across multiple consumer surveys. More practically, the uncollapsed desktop header consumes above-fold vertical space that should be occupied by the product image and purchase decision elements.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM + Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0
    Section 1 screenshot at scroll_y 0 shows the header has collapsed to a slim mobile bar with a hamburger (e0) and cart icon (e10/e11). However, the DOM slice for section 1 shows the full desktop three-column Bootstrap grid (col-sm-4 logo, col-sm-5 search + phone, col-sm-3 cart/login) in the header HTML, with no visible mobile breakpoint suppression of the phone number, search bar, and login links. The baton records e4 (logo img) at x=271, width=628 — these are DPR-scaled coordinates (3.0x), meaning the logo renders at approximately 209px CSS width centered in a 390px viewport, while the surrounding desktop header elements are either offscreen or crushed.
  • DOM · e0

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PU-04MEDIUMPerformance & UX
Hero Vehicle Selector Dropdowns — Four Sequential Decisions Before Finding Parts
The mobile vehicle selector is four sequential dropdowns (Make → Model → Year → Trim) — the highest-friction input pattern on mobile, where each tap opens an OS picker, the user makes a choice, and it closes before the next dropdown can be tapped. Four sequential decisions before the FIND PARTS button activates, which is exactly the kind of friction that drops users before they see a product.
What changes: Replace the four dropdowns with a single predictive search input (`Type your vehicle — 2018 Subaru WRX`) that resolves all four fields in one gesture with autocomplete. If the dropdown flow stays, default Make to your most-purchased brand to reduce active selections from 4 to 3.

Element

`banner` at e14 (vehicle selector with 4 dropdowns: Make, Model, Year, Trim)

Observation

The mobile hero presents four sequential dropdown selectors — Select Make, Select Model, Select Year, Select your Trim — as the primary above-fold CTA. On mobile, dropdowns are the highest-friction input pattern: each tap opens an OS-native picker, the user makes a selection, and the picker closes before the next dropdown can be used. Four sequential decisions must be completed in order before the FIND PARTS button activates. Hick's Law confirms that each additional required decision adds logarithmically to response time and cognitive cost.

Recommendation

Replace the four sequential dropdowns with a single predictive search input (`Type your vehicle — e.g., 2018 Subaru WRX`) that resolves Make/Model/Year/Trim in one gesture. A single text input with autocomplete triggers one keyboard interaction versus four dropdown interactions. Alternatively, if the dropdown flow is retained, default the Make selector to the most-purchased vehicle to reduce active selections from 4 to 3 for the majority of visitors.

Why this matters

The vehicle selector is the first conversion step for new visitors — an unsuccessful or frustrating vehicle selection experience drops users before they see a single product. Every additional mandatory decision corresponds to measurable drop-off; reducing sequential steps directly improves the rate at which visitors reach vehicle-filtered product listings.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e3
  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Header shows hamburger icon (top-left) and cart icon (top-right) as the only navigation affordances. No bottom navigation bar is present anywhere across the full page. The hamburger drawer contains Shop All, Shop by Category (sub-items: Performance, Exterior, Interior, Handling, Drivetrain, Brakes, Cooling, Wheels, Accessories), Shop by Vehicle (sub-items: Subaru WRX/STI, Ford Focus RS/ST), Contact, and My Account — all hidden behind a tap.

Source

Cognitive Load Management
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PU-05MEDIUMPerformance & UX
Hero Vehicle Selector Dropdowns — Four Sequential Decisions Before Finding Parts
The Speculation Rules API — which prerenders likely next pages so they appear instantly when a visitor clicks — isn't being used. Ray-Ban's controlled test found this single change produced +156% desktop and +101% mobile conversion on collection-to-PDP navigation. AWDMods' homepage has five high-confidence next destinations (the category CTAs) and 9 product links, which is an excellent fit for this pattern.
What changes: Add a `<script type='speculationrules'>` block targeting the five category collection URLs and the Featured Collection product URLs, with `eagerness: 'moderate'` so prerendering triggers on hover. Chrome-only, but ~65% of desktop traffic.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The mobile hero presents four sequential dropdown selectors — Select Make, Select Model, Select Year, and Select your Trim — as the primary above-fold CTA on the homepage. On mobile, dropdowns are the highest-friction input pattern: each tap opens an OS-native picker, the user makes a selection, and the picker closes before the next dropdown can be used. Four sequential decisions must be completed in order before the FIND PARTS button activates. Hick's Law confirms that each additional required decision adds logarithmically to response time and cognitive cost. The markup in the header DOM slice also shows the vehicle selector CTA ('SELECT YOUR VEHICLE / Car parts, simplified.') is hidden on mobile with class `hidden-xs`, confirming this is a mobile-specific UX issue where the four-dropdown flow is the intended primary mobile path.

Recommendation

If vehicle compatibility is the primary shopping path (which the information architecture confirms), consider replacing the four sequential dropdowns with a single predictive search input (type your vehicle — e.g., '2018 Subaru WRX') that resolves Make/Model/Year/Trim in one gesture. A single text input with autocomplete triggers one keyboard interaction versus four dropdown interactions, reducing the sequential decision chain from 4 steps to 1. Alternatively, if the dropdown flow is retained, default the Make selector to the most-purchased vehicle (Subaru WRX/STI or Ford Focus RS based on the store's catalog focus) to reduce the number of active selections from 4 to 3 for the majority of visitors.

Why this matters

The vehicle selector is the first conversion step for new visitors — an unsuccessful or frustrating vehicle selection experience drops users before they see a single product. Four dropdowns on a mobile screen represent the highest-friction variant of this flow. Every additional mandatory decision in the funnel corresponds to measurable drop-off; reducing sequential steps directly improves the rate at which visitors reach vehicle-filtered product listings.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e14
  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    The hero section shows four stacked dropdown selectors: Select Make, Select Model, Select Year, Select your Trim — each requiring a sequential decision before the blue FIND PARTS button activates. All four dropdowns are visible on the first mobile viewport at scroll_y=0.
