Research reference
GOLD
PU-06LOWPerformance & UX
Sticky Header Preserves Navigation Access on Scroll
The mobile header is sticky — the logo, hamburger, search, account, and cart icons all persist at the top of the viewport on every scroll position. The cart icon stays accessible no matter how far down the page a visitor goes.
What changes: No change needed. The remaining gap is that the hamburger is the only access to category browsing — see `performance-ux F-02`.

Element

`div` at e18 (`is_sticky: true`)

Observation

The sticky header element (e18) is confirmed sticky by both the captured element data and screenshots across sections 2, 3, and 4 — the header with logo, hamburger, account, and cart icons persists at the top of the viewport across all scroll positions. The cart icon (e6) is persistently accessible.

Recommendation

The sticky header is correctly implemented. The remaining gap — that the hamburger navigation is the only access point for category browsing — is addressed in `performance-ux F-02`.

Why this matters

Cart abandonment on mobile is 78–80%; maintaining persistent cart access reduces friction at the transition to checkout by keeping the cart icon visible at all times.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    The product image gallery shows a primary hero image at the top followed by a 2-row grid of 8 thumbnails (4 per row) plus a '+1 MORE' chip, plus prev/next chevron arrows at the sides of the main image. This is a hybrid thumbnail/chevron layout rather than a pure dot-indicator layout — the thumbnails are present but rendered as a dense 2-row grid rather than a single scrollable thumbnail strip below the hero.

Source

Mobile Conversion
BRONZEOpen primary source ↗
PP-01MEDIUMAfter Purchase
No Loyalty Program Teaser on Homepage
On mobile, the newsletter and footer carry no loyalty program awareness — no teaser in the newsletter, no Rewards link in the My Account accordion. Repeat customers visiting the homepage between orders have no reminder that a loyalty program exists, losing the goal-gradient effect that drives accelerated repeat purchase.
What changes: If a loyalty program exists, add an `Earn points on every order — join free` teaser above the newsletter email field and add a `Rewards` link to the footer My Account accordion. If no program exists, the newsletter is the natural anchor for a `Join Rewards` CTA.

Element

`(absent — no loyalty teaser in newsletter; no Rewards entry in footer My Account accordion)`

Observation

The AWDMods homepage carries no loyalty program signal anywhere — no points-teaser in the product area, no `Earn points on this purchase` copy in the newsletter section, no `Join Rewards` link in the footer `My Account` accordion. The footer accordion contains four links — My Account, Order History, Wish List, Track My Order — but no Rewards or Points entry.

Recommendation

If AWDMods has an active loyalty program, add an `Earn points on every order — join free` teaser to the newsletter section above the email input, and add a `Rewards` link to the footer `My Account` accordion. If no loyalty program exists yet, the newsletter section is the natural anchor for a `Join Rewards` enrollment CTA.

Why this matters

The confirmation page generates the highest loyalty enrollment rate (12-25% of new customers), but homepage awareness is what surfaces the program to returning visitors who missed the confirmation-page prompt. Without a loyalty signal on the homepage, repeat customers have no reminder that the program exists between orders.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=1629 (mobile)
    The newsletter section and footer contain no loyalty program teaser, no points-earned indicator, no 'join rewards' prompt, and no loyalty nav item. The footer 'My Account' accordion links to Order History and Track My Order but contains no loyalty or rewards entry point.

Source

Loyalty Programs
BRONZEOpen primary source ↗
PP-02LOWAfter Purchase
No Referral CTA in Footer or Newsletter Section
Neither the mobile newsletter nor the footer has a `Refer a Friend` mechanism. AWDMods' Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube social links are in the footer — proving social sharing infrastructure exists — but no structured referral incentive connects to that audience. The performance-mod community is intensely word-of-mouth driven; the absence of a referral program at the highest-trust acquisition channel is a meaningful forfeit.
What changes: Add a `Refer a Friend, Earn $X` link to the footer My Account accordion. Use give-first framing (`Give your friend $15 off. You get $15 too`) — outperforms get-first.

Element

`(absent — no referral link in footer; no `Share with a friend` in newsletter)`

Observation

Neither the newsletter section nor the footer contains a referral program CTA. The footer `My Account` accordion links to My Account, Order History, Wish List, and Track My Order — but no referral or `Share with a friend` entry point. AWDMods has Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube social links in the footer, confirming social sharing infrastructure exists, but no mechanism connects that social presence to a structured referral incentive.

Recommendation

Add a referral link to the footer `My Account` accordion — `Refer a Friend, Earn $X` — pointing to a referral landing page. Frame the CTA in give-first language: `Give your friend $15 off. You get $15 too.` If a referral program does not exist, build one — the performance-mod community is highly word-of-mouth driven, and the 72-hour post-purchase window is where referral participation peaks.

Why this matters

Word-of-mouth referrals from existing customers in niche automotive enthusiast communities carry outsized credibility compared to paid acquisition — referred customers arrive pre-validated by a trusted peer.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e13
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=2360 (mobile)
    Footer renders 'Reward Points' as a plain text link in the Information column, fourth item in an unordered list beneath 'Terms & Conditions'. No badge, no point value, no enrollment CTA accompanies the link. No visual differentiation from adjacent policy links.

Source

Referral Programs
SILVEROpen primary source ↗
PP-03MEDIUMAfter Purchase
No Post-Purchase Referral Prompt or Referral Program Surface
The desktop newsletter copy reads `New parts, new builds, exclusive deals. Be the first to know when something drops...`. Vehicle-specific (good) but framed entirely as broadcast announcements — no signal to the post-purchase customer that subscribing delivers ongoing value (build guides, owner tips, community content, reassurance during the shipping window).
What changes: Rewrite the subheadline to lead with subscriber benefit: `Get exclusive build guides, early access to new parts, and subscriber-only discounts for your Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI build.`

Element

(absent)

Observation

No referral program exists anywhere on this page. The footer Extras column, which is the most likely home for a site-level referral entry point, lists Gift Cards, Price Match Guarantee, and Dealer links but no referral offer. There is no 'Give a friend a discount' prompt, no referral link, and no share mechanism at any scroll depth. The page generates purchase intent but provides no structured pathway for buyers to amplify that intent through peer sharing.

Recommendation

At minimum, add a referral entry point to the Extras column in the footer: 'Refer a Friend — Give $15, Get $15.' This captures any post-purchase visitors who scroll to the footer. The higher-leverage placement is on the order confirmation page, where referral participation peaks in the 72-hour window after purchase. If a referral program is not yet built, a double-sided structure — equal reward for both the referrer and the referred friend — outperforms single-sided programs (52% vs 29% completion rate per Mention Me data). Copy framing should lead with giving: 'Give your friend $15 off their first Slingmods order' before stating the referrer's reward.

Why this matters

Referred customers from genuine peer referrals generate 25% higher profit margins over three years than customers acquired through paid channels (Villanueva et al., 2008, Journal of Marketing Research). For a specialty accessory retailer in a tight enthusiast community — Polaris Slingshot owners represent a defined, socially active group — referral is a particularly high-yield acquisition channel because product fit is pre-screened by the referring owner. Every month without a referral program is a month of social sharing momentum that cannot be recovered.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=2360 (mobile)
    Footer columns (Information, Year/Model Charts, Extras, My Account) contain no referral program link, no 'give a friend a discount' prompt, no share CTA of any kind. The Extras column lists Gift Cards and Price Match Guarantee but no referral offer.
  • Visual · section-5-mobile.jpg @ y=2800 (mobile)
    Lower footer confirms no referral entry point is present anywhere in the page footer or visible page sections.

Source

Research reference
BRONZE
PP-04MEDIUMAfter Purchase
No Referral CTA in Footer or Newsletter Section
The desktop footer and newsletter section have no referral entry point — no `Refer a Friend`, no `Give $X, Get $X`, no social-sharing mechanism connected to a structured incentive. Performance car modification is intensely social — owners share builds at events, on Instagram, on YouTube — and AWDMods has no path to convert that natural advocacy into measurable acquisition.
What changes: Add a `Refer a Friend` link to the footer My Account or Information column. In the newsletter section, add a one-line prompt below the email field: `Already a customer? Share AWDMods with a fellow RS or WRX owner — give them $15 off, get $15 back.`

Element

(absent)

Observation

Neither the newsletter section nor the footer contains a referral program CTA. The footer 'My Account' accordion links to My Account, Order History, Wish List, and Track My Order — but no referral or 'Share with a friend' entry point. AWDMods has Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube social links in the footer, which confirms social sharing infrastructure exists, but no mechanism connects that social presence to a structured referral incentive. On a homepage serving returning customers (who are already past the 72-hour post-purchase peak), the footer is the highest-reach location for a persistent referral CTA.

Recommendation

Add a referral link to the footer 'My Account' accordion — 'Refer a Friend, Earn $X' — pointing to a referral landing page. Frame the CTA in give-first language: 'Give your friend $15 off. You get $15 too.' If a referral program does not exist, this is the time to build one: the performance-mod community is highly word-of-mouth driven, and the 72-hour post-purchase window (order confirmation + shipping notification emails) is where referral participation peaks.

Why this matters

Word-of-mouth referrals from existing customers in niche automotive enthusiast communities (Focus RS/ST, WRX/STI owners) carry outsized credibility compared to paid acquisition — referred customers arrive pre-validated by a trusted peer. Without a referral mechanism, AWDMods forfeits the compounding acquisition benefit of enthusiast social networks at zero incremental media spend.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=1629 (mobile)
    Footer section shows Information and My Account collapsed accordions, social icons (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube), and payment method icons. No referral CTA, no 'Give a friend $X off' prompt, and no referral code share mechanism is present anywhere in the visible section.

Source

Research reference
BRONZE
PR-01HIGHPrice & Offers
Featured Card Price Has No MSRP or Comparison Anchor
On mobile, the $135.99 VelourTex floor mat displays as a single bare number with no MSRP, no `was/now` framing, no savings amount. Visitors have no signal for whether $135.99 is a fair price for a set of fitted floor mats — and a $135 utilitarian accessory evaluated in isolation feels expensive against the implicit $0 reference.
What changes: Show an MSRP strikethrough above the price, or a `Compare at` value documented from the previous selling price, so the card communicates value rather than just a number.

Element

`span.price` at e27 (content `From $135.99`)

Observation

Both visible featured-collection cards present bare selling prices with no reference anchor. The VelourTex floor mats show `FROM $135.99` — no MSRP strikethrough, no `was/now` framing, no savings percentage. The Revo Designs decal kit shows `$49.00` with identical absence of comparison context. Visitors land on these cards without a prior price signal; the advertised reference price mechanism (Grewal et al. 1998) that elevates perceived product value is entirely bypassed.

Recommendation

If either product has a manufacturer-suggested retail price above the selling price, render the MSRP as a grey strikethrough above the selling price and add a `Save $X (Y%)` line in the brand's accent color. Where a documented MSRP is unavailable, display a legitimate `regular price` based on actual prior pricing (FTC 16 CFR §233.1 requires the reference to reflect a bona fide prior offer). Liquid edit to the product card snippet that conditionally renders `compare_at_price` when populated.

Why this matters

Reference prices independently raise perceived product quality and transaction value before customers calculate savings — omitting them means $135.99 reads as expensive against nothing, suppressing click-through on the highest-ticket featured card.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e27
  • Visual · section-3-mobile.jpg @ y=917 (mobile)
    The VelourTex floor mat card displays 'FROM $135.99' in bold with no strikethrough MSRP, no 'compare at' price, and no savings amount or percentage. The partially visible second card (Revo Designs rocker stripes) shows '$49.00' with a star rating but also no anchor.

Source

Price Anchoring
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PR-02HIGHPrice & Offers
No MSRP Anchor on $69.95 Price Block
AWDMods sells multiple performance parts in the $400–$2,000 range — the Injen intake at $405, Borla exhaust at $1,650, Magnaflow exhaust at $1,766 — but offers no Buy Now Pay Later option anywhere on the site. Customers evaluating these as lump sums hit a much larger psychological barrier than they would seeing `4 payments of $441.50`. Shopify offers Shop Pay Installments natively at no extra merchant cost.
What changes: Enable Shop Pay Installments in the Shopify Payments settings, and surface the installment price beneath the selling price on every product card priced $200 and above.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The price block renders $69.95 as a standalone number — no MSRP strikethrough, no compare-at line, no was/now treatment. Without a reference price, visitors anchoring against alternatives they saw earlier (competitor listings, Google Shopping results, category-browse prices) carry those external anchors into the evaluation. A $69.95 price evaluated in isolation defaults to being judged against $0, which makes it feel expensive relative to the scale of the purchase decision. The CSS class price-old-live on e8 suggests the template expects a two-price display but no second price is populated.

Recommendation

If the manufacturer publishes an MSRP, display it as a strikethrough above the live price: MSRP $89.95 — Your Price $69.95. If no formal MSRP exists, document a defensible regular price based on actual prior pricing (FTC 16 CFR 233.1 requires bona fide prior retail pricing). As a fallback, frame the set value: 2 bags — $34.98 each above the bundle price to anchor value per-unit. The key mechanic is that the first price the visitor reads should be higher than $69.95.

Why this matters

Grewal et al. (1998) established that an advertised reference price simultaneously raises the internal reference price, perceived quality, and transaction value independent of the absolute discount. For a $69.95 specialty accessory — above the impulse threshold but below high-consideration — adding a credible MSRP anchor is the single highest-leverage pricing change available on this page. Real-world e-commerce A/B tests consistently show 5-15% conversion lifts from adding strikethrough pricing to pages that previously had none.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e7
  • DOM · e8
  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=829 (mobile)
    Price renders as a single bare number $69.95 with no strikethrough MSRP, no compare-at price, and no savings callout. The element carries the CSS class price-old-live but renders as the sole live price with no companion anchor number visible.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PR-03MEDIUMPrice & Offers
No BNPL Option on $135+ Featured Product
The free-shipping threshold sits in the announcement bar at the top of the page, but no individual product card communicates `Free shipping` at the moment a visitor is evaluating that specific item. Shipping ambiguity at the product stage is the top cited reason for cart abandonment in e-commerce.
What changes: Add a `Free shipping` sub-label below the price on cards for items that qualify, so the threshold becomes a per-item value signal rather than a top-of-page banner.

Element

(absent)

Observation

BNPL (Klarna, Afterpay, Affirm, or Shop Pay Installments) is absent throughout the site. The footer payment icons list eight payment methods and none of them is a BNPL provider. The featured $135.99 floor mat falls squarely within the $50–$500 range where BNPL generates its largest purchase-incidence and basket-size lift (Maesen & Ang 2025: ~9% purchase incidence increase, ~10% basket size increase). A 'Made to Order' product requiring upfront commitment is precisely the scenario where installment framing reduces perceived affordability barriers.

Recommendation

Add Shopify's native Shop Pay Installments (available at no extra merchant cost on eligible orders) or connect a third-party BNPL provider such as Afterpay. Once enabled, surface the installment price — e.g., '4 interest-free payments of $34.00' — directly beneath the selling price on product cards and PDPs. The installment amount, not the total, should be the psychologically salient figure while the full price remains visible for regulatory compliance.

Why this matters

Mid-ticket automotive accessories in the $100–$500 range are exactly where BNPL generates measurable conversion lift; leaving a $135 featured product without an installment option cedes that lift to competitors who display one.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e32
  • Visual · section-3-mobile.jpg @ y=917 (mobile)
    The VelourTex floor mat card shows 'FROM $135.99' with a 'Made to Order' badge. No Klarna, Afterpay, Affirm, or Shop Pay Installments widget appears on the card or anywhere in the featured collection section.
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=1629 (mobile)
    The footer payment icon row (e32) shows Amex, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, and Visa. No Klarna, Afterpay, or Affirm logo is present, confirming BNPL is not configured site-wide.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PR-04LOWPrice & Offers
Free Shipping Banner Uses Hedged Language That Dilutes Goal-Gradient Pull
The mobile free-shipping bar reads `FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+`. The dollar threshold is good, but `on most orders` introduces an exclusion the visitor cannot resolve — they don't know whether their item, size, or location qualifies. Vague threshold copy weakens the goal-gradient effect that motivates shoppers to add more to reach the threshold.
What changes: If standard-size orders all qualify, drop `most` and write `FREE SHIPPING on orders $75+`. If exclusions exist, link them in a tooltip rather than burying them in the headline.

Element

`banner` at e14 (y=0, content `FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+`)

Observation

The site-wide announcement bar reads `FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+`. The $75 threshold is a concrete anchor — that part is strong. However, `on most orders` hedges the promise without explanation: visitors don't know whether their item type, size, or location excludes them. The goal-gradient effect requires a perceived achievable goal; ambiguous eligibility disrupts goal formation.

Recommendation

Replace `on most orders $75+` with a cleaner threshold statement. If all standard-size orders qualify, write `FREE SHIPPING on orders $75+` with no qualifier. If exclusions exist (oversized parts, freight items), list them specifically in a tooltip or a linked page rather than burying them in the headline copy. A progress bar in the cart drawer showing exact distance to $75 would further operationalize the threshold.

Why this matters

Vague threshold copy undermines the $75 goal as a conversion lever; clarifying it costs a single copy edit and directly reduces the 39% of cart abandoners who cite unexpected shipping costs as a primary reason.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e13
  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=829 (mobile)
    The price block shows $69.95 and the Add to Cart button with no installment messaging below or adjacent to the price. No Affirm widget, no Pay in 4 callout, no monthly payment estimate appears near the price or CTA.
  • Visual · section-5-mobile.jpg @ y=2800 (mobile)
    Affirm logo appears in the footer payment icons row alongside Visa, Mastercard, and PayPal. The footer also contains an Affirm Monthly Payments link in the Information nav column. Affirm is enabled on the site but not surfaced at the price block where the purchase decision is made.

Source

Free Shipping
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PR-05LOWPrice & Offers
Charm Pricing Applied Consistently Across Featured Cards
The mobile cards use .99 charm pricing on the $135.99 floor mat — the right calibration for a utilitarian accessory at this price point. Decades of pricing research support .99 endings on non-luxury goods.
What changes: No change needed. As future variants and bundles are added, keep the .99 ending on items priced above $99 to preserve the left-digit effect.

Element

`span.price` at e27 (content `From $135.99`)

Observation

$135.99 uses a .99 ending on a utilitarian accessory (floor mats), appropriate for the product type and brand positioning. Troll & Wieseke (2024) meta-analysis confirms just-below pricing produces a statistically reliable positive effect on purchase intentions (d = 0.11) for non-luxury goods. $49.00 for the decal kit uses a round ending — also defensible, as round prices on smaller-ticket items reduce cognitive friction without the charm-pricing benefit being material at that price level.

Recommendation

No change required. The .99 ending on $135.99 is well-calibrated. If future variants or bundles are priced above $199, maintain the .99 or .95 ending to preserve the left-digit effect.

Why this matters

Maintaining charm pricing on mid-ticket utilitarian products is a low-cost optimization that preserves a small but real purchase-intention advantage.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=829 (mobile)
    Price block at scroll_y 829 shows $69.95 and the Add to Cart button with no shipping cost disclosed, no free-shipping badge, and no threshold progress bar adjacent to or below the price.
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=2500 (mobile)
    Footer asterisk text reads: * Free Shipping Applies to Most Contiguous U.S. Orders - Some Items May Not Qualify. This is small gray text in the footer disclaimer area, far below the purchase decision zone and only visible after substantial scrolling.

Source

Charm Pricing
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PR-06MEDIUMPrice & Offers
Free Shipping Banner Uses Hedged Language That Dilutes Goal-Gradient Pull
Most products in the Featured Collection use .99 endings ($135.99, $19.99, $59.99, $1,649.99) — but a few don't: $49.00, $1,766.00, and especially $405.48. The $405.48 is a mid-digit ending that gets neither the charm benefit (the left digit doesn't change) nor the round-number premium read.
What changes: Update the $405.48 SKU price to $399.99 (charm, left digit drops to 3) or $405.00 (clean round). Fix at the Shopify product level rather than the theme.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The site-wide announcement bar states 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+'. The $75 threshold is a concrete anchor — that part is strong. However, 'on most orders' hedges the promise without explanation: visitors don't know whether their item type, size, or location excludes them. The goal-gradient effect (Kivetz et al. 2006) requires a perceived achievable goal; ambiguous eligibility disrupts goal formation. A shopper building a $62 cart will only add $13 more if they believe their specific order qualifies.

Recommendation

Replace 'on most orders $75+' with a cleaner threshold statement. If all standard-size orders qualify, write 'FREE SHIPPING on orders $75+' with no qualifier. If exclusions exist (oversized parts, freight items), list them specifically in a tooltip or a linked page rather than burying them in the headline copy. A progress bar in the cart drawer showing exact distance to $75 would further operationalize the threshold into purchase-acceleration behavior.

Why this matters

Vague threshold copy undermines the $75 goal as a conversion lever; clarifying it costs a single copy edit and directly reduces the 39% of cart abandoners who cite unexpected shipping costs as a primary reason (Baymard Institute, current wave).

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e14
  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    The top announcement bar reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+' with a truck emoji. The phrase 'on most orders' qualifies the threshold, creating ambiguity about which orders actually qualify.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PR-07LOWPrice & Offers
Price Match Guarantee Exists but Not Shown at Price Block

Element

(absent)

Observation

Slingmods.com offers a Price Match Guarantee, but it is surfaced only as a footer link under Extras — not visible at the price block during the purchase decision. Shoppers evaluating a $69.95 specialty part have commonly compared prices on Amazon, competitor aftermarket sites, or Google Shopping before arriving. A price-match signal at the point of decision reduces the motivation to leave the page to complete those comparisons, because it communicates that no better price will be found elsewhere without consequence.

Recommendation

Add a Price Match Guarantee line or badge directly below the price or alongside the Add to Cart button. Minimal implementation: a single line of copy — Found it cheaper? We will match it. Price Match Guarantee — with a link to the guarantee terms. If mobile space is constrained, a small shield icon with a tooltip communicates the signal without adding visual bulk to the price block.

Why this matters

Jain and Srivastava (2000, JMR) found that price-match guarantees increase purchase intent and reduce price sensitivity even when customers almost never invoke them — the mechanism is that the guarantee signals pricing confidence and reduces the perceived need to comparison-shop. On a specialty aftermarket parts site where price-aware buyers routinely cross-check competitor retailers, surfacing the guarantee at the price block stops abandonment before the browser tab opens.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=829 (mobile)
    The price area shows only the bare $69.95, part number SM-27095, and a reward points line. No price-match badge or copy is visible at or near the price.
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=2400 (mobile)
    Under Extras in the footer nav, Price Match Guarantee appears as a text link. This is the only location on the page where the guarantee is surfaced.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-01HIGHProduct Photos & Video
Category Carousel Uses JS Scroll Component — No CSS Scroll-Snap
The mobile category carousel uses a custom JavaScript slider component instead of native CSS scroll-snap. JS scroll interception conflicts with mobile browsers' native touch handling — visitors trying to swipe horizontally accidentally scroll the page vertically, which produces a broken-feeling first impression on the homepage.
What changes: Replace the JS slider with CSS scroll-snap (`scroll-snap-type: x mandatory` on the container, `scroll-snap-align: start` on each slide). Hands swipe behavior to the browser's hardware-accelerated compositor thread.

Element

`slider-component` at e9 area (class `slider--scroll-active`; no scroll-snap declarations)

Observation

The category swipe carousel is powered by a custom `<slider-component>` JavaScript element (Shopify Dawn-derived). The DOM contains no `scroll-snap-type` or `scroll-snap-align` declarations. The class `slider--scroll-active` confirms JavaScript intercepts scroll events to manage swipe. JS scroll interception on mobile creates friction when users attempt natural swipe gestures and produces less-smooth animation than hardware-accelerated CSS scroll-snap.

Recommendation

Replace the JS scroll-interception approach with CSS scroll-snap on the carousel list: add `scroll-snap-type: x mandatory` to the `<ul>` container and `scroll-snap-align: start` to each `<li>` slide. This hands swipe behavior to the browser compositor thread, eliminating JS scroll-conflict jank on iOS Safari and Android Chrome. The existing `slider-button` prev/next controls can remain as progressive enhancement.

Why this matters

Mobile users expect swipe gestures to work like native app behavior. JavaScript scroll interception is the most common cause of broken swipe patterns on mobile — it causes scroll-direction conflicts that result in accidental page scroll instead of card swipe. A broken swipe experience on the homepage category carousel creates an immediate negative first impression.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Gallery renders as a 2-row, 4-column static thumbnail grid below the hero image. No horizontal swipe container is present. Users on mobile must tap arrow buttons (<, >) to navigate rather than swiping the hero image naturally. The grid layout is not a scroll-snap strip and does not support native swipe-to-advance on the hero.

Source

Thumbnail Design
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
PM-02MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Category Carousel Uses JS Scroll Component — No CSS Scroll-Snap
Desktop product cards show one static image with no hover interaction — no image swap, no lifestyle-vs-packshot reveal, no quick-action overlay. Baymard's benchmark identifies this as the highest single-interaction ROI enhancement for desktop product listings, and 76% of sites fail to implement it.
What changes: Add a CSS opacity-transition hover swap (150–200ms ease) that replaces the primary image with a secondary angle on `mouseenter`. Preload the secondary image for above-fold cards. Add a quick-add or `View Details` button visible on hover.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The category swipe carousel is powered by a custom <slider-component> JavaScript element (Shopify Dawn-derived). The DOM contains no scroll-snap-type or scroll-snap-align declarations. The class slider--scroll-active confirms JavaScript intercepts scroll events to manage swipe. JS scroll interception on mobile creates friction when users attempt natural swipe gestures — conflicting with the page's native vertical scroll — and produces less-smooth animation than hardware-accelerated CSS scroll-snap.

Recommendation

Replace the JS scroll-interception approach with CSS scroll-snap on the carousel list: add scroll-snap-type: x mandatory to the <ul> container and scroll-snap-align: start to each <li> slide. This hands swipe behavior to the browser compositor thread, eliminating JS scroll-conflict jank on iOS Safari and Android Chrome. The existing slider-button prev/next controls can remain as progressive enhancement for users who prefer arrow navigation.

Why this matters

Mobile users expect swipe gestures to work like native app behavior. JavaScript scroll interception is the most common cause of broken swipe patterns on mobile — it causes scroll-direction conflicts that result in accidental page scroll instead of card swipe. A broken swipe experience on the homepage category carousel creates an immediate negative first impression that degrades trust before users have browsed a single product.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e9
    The category carousel is implemented as a <slider-component> custom element with class slider--scroll-active. No scroll-snap-type or scroll-snap-align properties are present in the inline styles or in the component-slider.css link. The Shopify Dawn-derived slider component uses JavaScript scroll interception rather than native CSS scroll-snap. The class slider--scroll-active confirms JS-controlled scroll is active.
  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=650 (mobile)
    Category carousel on mobile. The partial visibility of a second card at the right edge suggests horizontal scrollability, but the implementation relies on a custom JS slider component rather than CSS scroll-snap.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-03MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
No Product Demo or Installation Video in Gallery
Desktop product cards have a split hit area — the image links to the PDP, but the title, price, and metadata below aren't part of the same clickable region. Baymard finds this pattern at 76% of sites and identifies it as a consistent source of misclicks: visitors moving from the title text toward an adjacent card frequently click the wrong product.
What changes: Wrap the entire card surface — image, title, brand, price, badge, rating — in a single `<a>` element pointing to the PDP. Position separate-action elements (wishlist, quick-add) with absolute positioning and higher z-index above the link layer.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The gallery contains 9 still photographs — installation-in-vehicle shots and standalone bag views — but no video. This is a product with clear video ROI triggers: it involves physical installation into Polaris Slingshot rear storage compartments, it has fit-and-function behavior (zipper operation, carry handle, how the bags slide in and out), and the target buyer is a powersports enthusiast who needs confidence the bags will actually work in their specific model year. A 30–60 second installation or in-use demonstration video placed at gallery position 2 or 3 would address the most common pre-purchase hesitation for a fitment accessory at this price point.

Recommendation

Produce a 30–60 second usage/demonstration video showing the bags being installed in the Slingshot rear compartments, the zippers and handles in operation, and the bags filled with gear. Place it as gallery position 2 (immediately after the hero lifestyle shot). Add a video thumbnail with a centered play button overlay — do not use a black frame or the EvolutionR logo as the poster. For the mobile player: implement `playsinline preload="metadata"` attributes and keep the file under 5MB for 4G delivery. Add VideoObject JSON-LD with `name`, `thumbnailUrl`, `uploadDate`, and `duration` fields.

Why this matters

Fitment accessories like storage bags live or die on 'will this actually work in my vehicle' confidence. Still photos show the product installed, but they cannot convey ease of installation, zipper smoothness, or how the carry handles feel under load. A usage video is the most direct substitute for handling the product in person, and its absence leaves buyers to rely solely on the fitment chart and written description — a higher-friction path to purchase.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Gallery contains 9 still images (8 visible thumbnails + 1 more). No video thumbnail with a play button overlay is present at any gallery position. No video section appears elsewhere in the visible page sections.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-04MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
No In-Scale Image Showing Bag Size Reference

Element

(absent)

Observation

All 9 gallery images show the bags in the Slingshot's rear compartments or as standalone objects. There is no image showing a hand holding a bag, no ruler, and no common-scale reference. The spec table on the product page lists Color, Material, and UV Protection, but omits bag dimensions entirely. A buyer unfamiliar with the Slingshot's rear compartment volume cannot assess whether these bags will accommodate their specific packing needs — gloves, tools, a phone charger, or a change of clothes — from any of the current media assets.

Recommendation

Add one image showing a bag held open alongside a recognizable object that communicates scale — a pair of gloves, a compact helmet, or a 12-inch ruler held next to the open bag. Alternatively, photograph a bag next to a hand for immediate human-scale reference. Include bag dimensions (L x W x D) in the product spec table adjacent to the existing Color and Material fields. If dimensions are unavailable from the manufacturer, an installed-weight or capacity metric (e.g., 'fits up to a medium Slingshot helmet') would serve a similar role.

Why this matters

Storage accessories bought without a size reference generate 'not as expected' returns when the delivered product is larger or smaller than imagined. For a $69.95 set-of-two purchase, a return driven by size confusion costs more in logistics than the photography fix.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    All 9 gallery images show the bags installed in the Slingshot compartment or as standalone objects against a neutral/dark background. No image includes a hand, common object, or ruler providing a recognizable size reference for the bags themselves.
  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=700 (mobile)
    The product spec table lists no dimensions. The description in section 3 mentions the bags fill the factory rear storage compartments but provides no L x W x D measurement and no in-scale imagery.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-05LOWProduct Photos & Video
All Category Card Background Images Have Empty Alt Text

Element

(absent)

Observation

Every background image in the five category tiles (Performance, Handling & Brakes, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) carries alt="". While empty alt is technically valid for decorative images, these images depict the product category content and serve as the primary visual anchor for each tile. Screen reader users navigating the carousel receive no image description — the card is announced solely by the heading text and subcategory list.

Recommendation

Add descriptive alt text to each category card image that describes the visual content shown, not just repeats the heading. For example: the Performance card image showing a turbocharger and intake manifold should read alt="Turbocharger and cold air intake performance parts" rather than alt="Performance" (which duplicates the heading). This passes WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content and provides useful context to users on slow connections where the image may not load.

Why this matters

WCAG 2.1 Level A requires informative images to have descriptive alt text. The EU European Accessibility Act (effective June 28, 2025) now makes accessibility non-compliance a legal exposure for EU-facing merchants. Beyond compliance, image alt text on category cards is indexed by Google Image Search — accurate descriptions contribute to organic search visibility for category-level queries.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e8
    All five category card images in the multicolumn carousel have alt="" (empty string): verified across all five <img> tags in the section-1 DOM slice. The images show performance parts, brakes, interior components, exterior styling, and electronics — descriptive alt text would benefit both screen readers and image-search indexing.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
PM-06LOWProduct Photos & Video
Thumbnail Grid Shows 8 of 9 Images — '+1 MORE' Is Low-Visibility

Element

(absent)

Observation

The gallery communicates 9 total images by showing 8 thumbnails plus a '+1 MORE' badge in a small gray pill below the grid. The '+1 MORE' treatment partially satisfies the image-count signal requirement, but the badge reads as a passive label rather than an interactive control — its tap target area is small (approximately 70x22px at 1x CSS, well under the 44px Apple HIG minimum), and its gray fill on white background is low-contrast. Users may read it as an informational note rather than a button that opens the hidden ninth image.

Recommendation

If the gallery is rebuilt as a horizontal swipe strip per Finding F-1, the count badge becomes the natural scroll-to-end signal and should display as '1 of 9' updating as the user swipes. If the grid layout is retained, replace the '+1 MORE' pill with a tappable '+1 more image' link styled at minimum 44px tap target height in the brand's active link color (currently rgb(80,80,80) — consider a higher-contrast treatment). Ensure the ninth image is reachable via a single tap.

Why this matters

A hidden gallery image that cannot be easily discovered represents a wasted photography asset. For a buyer evaluating a $69.95 purchase, the final image may contain the most persuasive angle — and if it is behind a low-visibility control, it might as well not exist.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    The thumbnail grid shows 8 images in two rows of four. A gray pill-shaped badge reading '+1 MORE' appears below the grid at small size (approximately 70px wide). The badge is low-contrast gray-on-white and visually passive — it does not clearly function as a navigation tap target or signal what the hidden image contains.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
TC-01HIGHTrust & Credibility
Featured Product Has 2 Reviews at Perfect 5.0 — Below Threshold and Suspect Rating
Two issues on the same mobile featured row: the Revo Designs card shows a perfect 5.0 from only 2 reviews (a skepticism trigger), and the $135.99 VelourTex floor mat shows no rating at all. The highest-priced card on the homepage has zero visible social proof, and the next card looks suspicious.
What changes: Run a post-purchase review request for both products. For VelourTex specifically, hide the empty star widget rather than displaying a blank — and prioritize getting it past 5 reviews, where the conversion lift on higher-priced items is most pronounced.

Element

`div.rating` at e26 (content `5.0 / 5.0 (2) 2 total reviews`)

Observation

The Revo Designs Rocker Stripes Decal Kit card displays a rating of 5.0 / 5.0 derived from exactly 2 reviews. This is below the 5-review threshold where purchase likelihood jumps 270% (Spiegel Research Center, Northwestern), and a perfect 5.0 rating is the precise configuration that triggers skepticism — Spiegel's research confirms purchase likelihood peaks at 4.0–4.7 and decreases as ratings approach 5.0. The VelourTex Carpet Floor Mats card (`From $135.99`) displays no rating or review count whatsoever, leaving a $135.99+ product with zero social proof on the homepage.

Recommendation

For the Revo Designs card: implement a post-purchase email review request triggered by delivery confirmation, sent 7–14 days post-delivery, targeting all buyers of this SKU. The goal is reaching 5 verified reviews — at that threshold the conversion lift is documented at 270%. Do not artificially inflate the rating by soliciting only satisfied customers; the 4.0–4.7 range converts better than 5.0. For the VelourTex card: this higher-AOV product receives a 380% conversion lift from reviews per Spiegel's price-segmented data — prioritize review collection for this SKU. If zero reviews exist, suppress the star widget entirely rather than showing an empty state.

Why this matters

Products under 5 reviews lose the majority of the conversion lift that social proof provides. On a homepage featuring only a handful of products, a 2-review card with a suspicious perfect score actively undermines the credibility AWDMods needs as an independent specialty retailer competing without Amazon's brand equity.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e26
  • Visual · section-3-mobile.jpg @ y=917 (mobile)
    The second featured product card (Revo Designs Rocker Stripes Decal Kit) shows a 5-star rating widget with a perfect 5.0 score based on only 2 reviews. The first featured card (VelourTex Floor Mats, From $135.99) shows no star rating or review count at all.

Source

Trust And Credibility
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
TC-02HIGHTrust & Credibility
No Trust Badges Near Any CTA — Payment Icons Confined to Footer Only
On mobile, payment-method logos appear only at the very bottom of the page, far below where purchase decisions actually happen. PayPal, Shop Pay, and other recognized marks should be visible near the product cards and inside the cart drawer — at the moment a shopper is deciding to buy — not buried in the footer where most mobile visitors never scroll.
What changes: Add a small trust strip with 2–3 marks (PayPal, Shop Pay, plus a free-shipping or returns badge) directly beneath the Featured Collection. Don't replicate the full 8-icon footer grid; a few well-chosen marks at the conversion point outperform many marks in the footer.

Element

`list` at e32 (y=1629, content `American Express Apple Pay Discover Google Pay Mastercard...`)

Observation

Across all four page sections, payment-method icons appear exclusively in the footer (e32, scroll_y 1629+). No security seal, money-back guarantee, or return policy indicator is present near any product card, above-fold CTA, or add-to-cart action. Baymard's research confirms trust badges placed in headers or footers are perceived as generic and do not convey that the payment process specifically is secure — the proximity effect requires placement at the moment of purchasing anxiety. PayPal (67% eye-tracking notice rate, CXL Institute) is present in the footer icon row but receives no attention-driving placement where purchase decisions are made.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust bar — 2–3 badges maximum — directly beneath the product cards in the Featured Collection section, where a shopper's next action is clicking through to purchase. The bar should include PayPal (already accepted, highest recognition) and one additional signal: either a `Free Shipping $75+` reassurance badge or a `30-Day Returns` badge if that policy exists. Do not replicate the full footer payment grid; 2–3 well-chosen marks at the conversion point outperform a grid of 8 icons confined to the footer.

Why this matters

AWDMods is an independent specialty retailer without the brand recognition of Amazon or AutoZone. For this audience segment, trust signals must appear where purchase decisions occur. Footer-only placement means shoppers who do not scroll to the bottom — the majority on mobile — never encounter a trust signal before bouncing.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM + Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=779
    Star rating row renders five hollow (empty) stars with '(0 reviews)' label adjacent to an 'IN STOCK' badge. No review count, no average rating, no social proof of any kind.
  • DOM · e5

Source

Trust And Credibility
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
TC-03MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
No Trust Badges Near Any CTA — Payment Icons Confined to Footer Only
Most of the Featured Collection's highest-priced products show no reviews at all. The Borla exhaust at $1,650, Magnaflow at $1,766, and Injen intake at $405 are exactly the items where buyers want peer validation before clicking. Right now there's none.
What changes: Treat the Featured Collection as a curated trust showcase: only feature products that have reached 5+ reviews, or run a focused post-purchase review campaign on the high-AOV items. Even 5 honest reviews on a $1,650 exhaust have an outsized conversion impact.

Element

(absent)

Observation

Across all four page sections, payment-method icons appear exclusively in the footer (e32, scroll_y 1629+). No security seal, money-back guarantee, or return policy indicator is present near any product card, above-fold CTA, or add-to-cart action. Baymard's research confirms that trust badges placed in headers or footers are perceived as generic and do not convey that the payment process specifically is secure — the proximity effect requires placement at the moment of purchasing anxiety, not at the page extremities. PayPal (67% eye-tracking notice rate, CXL Institute) is present in the footer icon row but receives no attention-driving placement where purchase decisions are made.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust bar — 2–3 badges maximum — directly beneath the product cards in the Featured Collection section, where a shopper's next action is clicking through to purchase. The bar should include PayPal (already accepted, highest recognition) and one additional signal: either a 'Free Shipping $75+' reassurance badge (the threshold is already promoted in the announcement bar) or a '30-Day Returns' badge if that policy exists. Do not replicate the full footer payment grid; 2–3 well-chosen marks at the conversion point outperform a grid of 8 icons confined to the footer.

Why this matters

AWDMods is an independent specialty retailer without the brand recognition of Amazon or AutoZone. For this audience segment, trust signals must appear where purchase decisions occur. Footer-only placement means shoppers who do not scroll to the bottom — the majority on mobile — never encounter a trust signal before bouncing.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e32
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=1629 (mobile)
    Payment method icons (Amex, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) appear only in the footer at the very bottom of the page. No security seal, money-back guarantee badge, or return policy statement appears anywhere in the above-fold area, near any product card, or near any CTA button across all four sections.

Source

Research reference
GOLD
TC-04MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
Return Policy Absent From Homepage — No Visible Link or Snippet in Any Section
On mobile, the `Information` accordion in the footer is collapsed by default — meaning any return-policy link inside is invisible without an extra tap. Nothing on the page tells a first-time visitor whether they can return a product if it doesn't fit. For automotive parts where fitment errors are real risk, this absence directly suppresses purchase intent.
What changes: Surface a one-line `Free returns within 30 days` near the Featured Collection or in a global trust bar below the navigation. Either expand the footer Information accordion by default, or pull the return-policy link out where it's visible without tapping.

Element

`contentinfo` at e30 (y=1461, content `Information About Us`)

Observation

The footer's `Information` accordion is collapsed on mobile (e30, scroll_y 1461+), meaning any return or shipping policy links inside are invisible without a tap interaction. No return policy text, summary badge, or `Easy Returns` indicator appears in any of the four page sections. Google's Quality Rater Guidelines identify a clearly stated return policy linked from the product page as a required Trustworthiness element for high-Trust ratings.

Recommendation

If AWDMods offers returns, surface a one-line policy summary — `Free returns within 30 days` or equivalent — as a text line or small badge near the featured product cards. Add to the same trust bar recommended in `trust-credibility F-03`. Additionally, expand the footer Information accordion by default on mobile, or ensure return/shipping links appear as visible text rather than behind a collapsed interaction.

Why this matters

First-time visitors to an independent automotive parts retailer carry meaningful purchase risk anxiety — incorrect fitment or product dissatisfaction has no obvious resolution if no return policy is visible. Eliminating that anxiety before the click-through to a product page reduces friction at the highest-dropout stage of the funnel.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=900 (mobile)
    The Add to Cart button area shows the price ($69.95), quantity selector, and 'Add to Cart' button. No trust badges, no security seals, no returns/guarantee callout, no payment method icons are present in this section. The payment method logos appear only in the footer at scroll_y 2360+.

Source

Trust And Credibility
BRONZEOpen primary source ↗
TC-05MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
No Expert Editorial Content — Homepage Carries Only Category Navigation and Product Cards
On mobile, the homepage shows category navigation and product cards but no editorial voice — no `Our Expert Take`, no fitment notes, no signal that AWDMods knows the platforms it serves. Performance buyers research extensively and trust focused retailers over generalists; without an expert voice, AWDMods reads identical to a generic drop-shipper aggregating the same catalog.
What changes: Add a 3–4 sentence expert-voice block beneath the Featured Collection: name the brand's focus (`We specialize in Focus RS/ST and WRX/STI builds — every part is one we've tested`) plus 1–2 staff-pick callouts with a specific fitment note. Copy change, no UI redesign.

Element

`(absent — proposed location: 3-4 sentence expert-voice block beneath Featured Collection)`

Observation

Across all four page sections, no expert editorial content is present: no staff picks with fitment rationale, no `vehicles we know best` copy, no technical orientation for new visitors. The homepage communicates category breadth and a vehicle finder, but nothing that demonstrates AWDMods' deep platform knowledge of the Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI vehicles it explicitly names in the newsletter copy. Google's Quality Rater Guidelines identify expert editorial content — fitment compatibility notes, installation difficulty ratings, known issues the manufacturer omits — as a key Expertise signal that differentiates a focused retailer from a generic reseller.

Recommendation

Add a short expert-voice block beneath the Featured Collection section — 3–4 sentences establishing AWDMods' focus (`We specialize in Focus RS/ST and WRX/STI builds; every part we carry is one we've tested or installed ourselves`) alongside 1–2 staff-pick callouts for currently featured products that name a specific fitment or installation note. Copy change, not a UI redesign. For technically complex products (exhausts, intakes, suspension), brief fitment notes (`Fits Focus RS 2016–2018 with stock subframe`) are the clearest Expertise signal possible.

Why this matters

Without editorial voice, AWDMods looks identical to a generic drop-shipper aggregating the same catalog. Automotive performance shoppers research extensively and trust specialists over generalists — demonstrating domain knowledge on the homepage is the differentiator that earns the product-page click from a comparison shopper.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=900 (mobile)
    The entire Add to Cart zone — quantity selector, ATC button, 'Add to Wishlist' link, and product spec table — contains no return policy mention. Terms & Conditions appears only as a footer link at the bottom of the page.
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=2360 (mobile)
    Footer Information section lists 'Terms & Conditions' as a link. No returns or refunds policy link is surfaced separately. No return policy summary is visible anywhere in the product content.

Source

Trust And Credibility
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
TC-06HIGHTrust & Credibility
Return Policy Absent From Homepage — No Visible Link or Snippet in Any Section
The footer's Information column has About Us, Privacy Policy, Terms, and CARB Policy — but no Return Policy or Shipping Policy link. For visitors evaluating a `Made to Order` exhaust system at $1,650, the absence of a visible return path resolves to `probably non-refundable` and suppresses purchase intent.
What changes: Add a `Returns & Shipping` link to the footer Information column. For higher impact, surface a one-line `Free returns within 30 days on most orders` near the Featured Collection itself.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The footer's 'Information' accordion is collapsed on mobile (e30, scroll_y 1461+), meaning any return or shipping policy links inside are invisible without a tap interaction. No return policy text, summary badge, or 'Easy Returns' indicator appears in any of the four page sections. Google's Quality Rater Guidelines identify a clearly stated return policy linked from the product page as a required Trustworthiness element for high-Trust ratings — its absence is a gap in both conversion trust signals and E-E-A-T evaluation.

Recommendation

If AWDMods offers returns, surface a one-line policy summary — 'Free returns within 30 days' or equivalent — as a text line or small badge near the featured product cards. This can be added to the same trust bar recommended in Finding 2 (F-02). Additionally, expand the footer Information accordion by default on mobile, or ensure return/shipping links appear as visible text rather than behind a collapsed interaction. The goal is that a first-time visitor can confirm a return path exists without needing to hunt.

Why this matters

First-time visitors to an independent automotive parts retailer carry meaningful purchase risk anxiety — incorrect fitment or product dissatisfaction has no obvious resolution if no return policy is visible. Eliminating that anxiety before the click-through to a product page reduces friction at the highest-dropout stage of the funnel.

  • DOM · e30
  • Visual · section-4-mobile.jpg @ y=1629 (mobile)
    Footer shows two collapsed accordion sections — 'Information' and 'My Account' — with no visible return policy link or summary. No return policy snippet appears near any product card or CTA. The baton's footer element (e30) text content shows only 'Information About Us', with the return/shipping policy content hidden behind collapsed accordions on mobile.

Research reference
GOLD
TC-07MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
No Expert Editorial Content — Homepage Carries Only Category Navigation and Product Cards
Product images on the desktop Featured Collection have descriptive alt text that names the product, vehicle, and variant — meeting accessibility standards and supporting Google Image indexing. This is correctly implemented.
What changes: No change needed. Maintain this alt-text standard as new products are added to the catalog.

Element

(absent)

Observation

Across all four page sections, no expert editorial content is present: no staff picks with fitment rationale, no 'vehicles we know best' copy, no technical orientation for new visitors. The homepage communicates category breadth (Performance, Handling, Interior, Exterior, Electronics) and a vehicle finder, but nothing that demonstrates AWDMods' specialist knowledge of the Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, or STI platforms it explicitly names in the newsletter copy. Google's Quality Rater Guidelines identify expert editorial content — fitment compatibility notes, installation difficulty ratings, known issues the manufacturer omits — as a key Expertise signal that differentiates a specialist from a generic reseller.

Recommendation

Add a short specialist-voice block beneath the Featured Collection section — 3–4 sentences establishing AWDMods' focus ('We specialize in Focus RS/ST and WRX/STI builds; every part we carry is one we've tested or installed ourselves') alongside 1–2 staff-pick callouts for currently featured products that name a specific fitment or installation note the product card omits. This is a copy change, not a UI redesign, and it serves both the first-time visitor who wants assurance they're on the right site and Google's quality raters who look for genuine product knowledge. For technically complex products (exhausts, intakes, suspension), brief fitment notes ('Fits Focus RS 2016–2018 with stock subframe') are the clearest Expertise signal possible.

Why this matters

Without editorial voice, AWDMods looks identical to a generic drop-shipper aggregating the same catalog. Automotive performance shoppers research extensively and trust specialists over generalists — demonstrating domain knowledge on the homepage is the differentiator that earns the product-page click from a comparison shopper.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Above-fold content is a vehicle fitment finder (Select Make / Model / Year / Trim) and a category carousel. No 'Our Expert Take', 'Staff Picks', fitment notes, or specialist editorial copy appears anywhere on the page across all four sections.

Source

Research reference
SILVER
TC-08LOWTrust & Credibility
Product Images Carry Descriptive Alt Text — Accessibility Baseline Met
Product images in the mobile Featured Collection carry full descriptive alt text following a clean `[Brand] [Product Name] [Fitment]` template. This passes WCAG accessibility requirements and supports Google Image indexing.
What changes: No change needed. Lock this alt-text pattern at the product-data level so new SKUs inherit it automatically.

Element

`img` at e22 (alt `VelourTex Fitted Carpet Floor Mats for the Ford Focus RS / ST (Set of 4)`)

Observation

All four featured product card images (e22–e25) carry descriptive alt text that names the product, compatible vehicle, and variant detail. This matches the recommended formula (`[Product Name] — [Variant/Context]`) and passes the WebAIM Million benchmark that 53.1% of pages fail.

Recommendation

Maintain this standard. As inventory expands, enforce the alt text template at the product data level so new SKUs inherit descriptive text automatically rather than requiring post-upload edits.

Why this matters

Descriptive alt text serves screen reader users, improves image SEO indexability, and is among the lowest-effort accessibility wins available.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=200 (mobile)
    Product gallery shows 9 professional product photographs (bags on white background, bags installed in Slingshot, detail shots). No customer-submitted photos are present in the gallery or in any review section visible on the page. The Reviews tab in the tab bar shows '(0)' confirming zero review-sourced content exists.

Source

Accessibility
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
TC-09LOWTrust & Credibility
OnlineStore Schema Incomplete — Missing AggregateRating and Physical Address

Element

(absent)

Observation

The page head carries two JSON-LD blocks: an OnlineStore schema and a WebSite schema with SearchAction. The OnlineStore schema includes the site name, logo URL, and social sameAs links (Facebook, Instagram, YouTube), but omits a physical address, telephone, and email — three elements Google's Quality Rater Guidelines identify as required Trustworthiness signals for unknown brands. Several sameAs entries are empty strings ('', '', '', '', '', '', '', ''), suggesting incomplete setup. No AggregateRating schema is present at the store or product level.

Recommendation

Complete the OnlineStore schema: add a 'address' property with PostalAddress (streetAddress, addressLocality, addressRegion, postalCode, addressCountry), a 'telephone' property, and a 'contactPoint' or 'email'. Remove the empty-string sameAs entries — they add no value and may signal incomplete implementation to quality raters. On product pages, add AggregateRating schema once review counts meet the 5-review threshold. The SearchPilot controlled test cited in ugc-reviews-seo.md found ~20% organic traffic uplift from adding AggregateRating schema alone.

Why this matters

Schema markup makes existing trust signals machine-readable for Google. A physical address in structured data directly satisfies the Trustworthiness pillar that quality raters evaluate for unknown brands. Empty sameAs fields are a low-signal indicator that the schema was partially configured and abandoned — a detail quality raters notice.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e30

Source

Research reference
GOLD
VC-01HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
No Above-Fold Headline or Value Proposition
On mobile, the entire 390×844 above-fold viewport shows a dark car photograph, four unlabeled dropdowns, and a `FIND PARTS` button — but no headline. A first-time visitor scrolling past this in 2 seconds has no clue what AWDMods sells or why they should care. The result is preventable bounce.
What changes: Add a one-line headline above the vehicle selector — `Performance Parts for Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, and STI`. Costs no additional viewport height if line-height is constrained.

Element

`(absent — proposed location: above the vehicle selector dropdowns in the hero)`

Observation

The entire above-fold viewport on mobile (390×844px) contains no headline and no value proposition. A visitor arriving cold sees a dark car photograph, four unlabeled dropdown selectors, and a blue `FIND PARTS` button — but nothing that answers `What does this store sell?` or `Why should I choose AWDMods over a competitor?` The page title `AWDMods - AWDMods` appears only in the browser tab. Baymard and NNGroup research identifies a visible above-fold headline as a required element for landing pages receiving cold or paid traffic.

Recommendation

Add a single concise headline directly above or beside the vehicle-selector widget — `Performance Parts for Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, and STI` — that identifies the store's product scope. If the brand positioning warrants differentiation, append `Expert-curated. Ships fast.` This can be a one-line `<h1>` or `<p>` element that sits above the selector dropdowns within the existing hero layout, costing zero additional viewport height if line-height is constrained.

Why this matters

Without a visible headline, every cold-traffic visitor must decode the page's purpose from visual cues alone. At mobile scroll speeds, many will bounce before the vehicle selector registers as a tool rather than a form to ignore. A single descriptive headline resolves the `Is this the right place?` question in under 2 seconds.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    The above-fold viewport at scroll_y=0 shows a dark hero image of a Subaru WRX with vehicle selector dropdowns (Select Make, Select Model, Select Year, Select Trim) and a blue FIND PARTS button. No headline or value proposition text is present anywhere in this viewport — only the AWDMods logo and a free-shipping banner at the top.

Source

Hero Section Psychology
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-02HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
Product Cards Lack Visible Add-to-Cart CTA
On mobile, the only purchase action on each product card is a tiny (~40×40px) orange `+` icon at the bottom corner of the image — below the WCAG accessible touch-target minimum and unlabeled. A visitor cannot tell whether tapping it adds to cart, opens a configurator, or navigates to the PDP without trying it.
What changes: Add a clearly labeled, near-full-width CTA button beneath the price on every product card — `Add to Cart` or `Choose Options` for `Made to Order` items — at minimum 48px tall.

Element

`img` at e22 (y=3000, 798×798 device px) — the `+` overlay is a 40×40 device px circular icon at bottom-right

Observation

The VelourTex Floor Mats card in the Featured Collection presents a product image, title, price (`FROM $135.99`), and a `Made to Order` badge — but the only purchasable action is a small (~40×40px) orange circular `+` icon at the bottom corner of the product image. This icon is below the WCAG 2.2 AA 44×44px touch target recommendation and is unlabeled — a visitor cannot tell by looking whether it adds to cart, opens a configurator, or navigates to the PDP. NNGroup research on CTA label clarity shows unlabeled or ambiguously-labeled primary actions produce lower engagement.

Recommendation

Add a clearly labeled, full-width or near-full-width CTA button beneath the price line on each product card — `Add to Cart` for in-stock products or `Choose Options` for products requiring configuration (the `Made to Order` floor mats). The button should use the same blue fill currently used on category CTAs, be at minimum 48px tall, and carry a text label visible at rest state without any hover or tap interaction. The small orange `+` can remain as a supplementary quick-add shortcut but should not be the only purchase affordance.

Why this matters

Product cards on a homepage Featured Collection are a direct revenue path — a visitor browsing with purchase intent should be able to initiate the cart action without navigating to a PDP first. An unlabeled icon button as the sole card-level CTA forces every interested visitor through an extra tap, adding friction at the highest-intent moment in the browse flow.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=700 (mobile)
    The Add to Cart button is visible only within the product info zone (approx. scroll_y 700-950). Below that, the page continues with fitment tables, description, and feature bullets with no persistent CTA. No sticky bottom bar is present at any scroll depth visible in the captured screenshots.

Source

CTA Design And Placement
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-03MEDIUMHeadlines & Buttons
No Trust Signal in Above-Fold Hero
On mobile, the above-fold area has no trust signal at all. The free-shipping bar communicates a policy but not credibility — it doesn't answer `Can I trust this merchant?` for a first-time visitor. Cold-traffic visitors arriving from a paid ad land on a dark unfamiliar site with a vehicle form and no reassurance.
What changes: Add a one-line trust element beneath the vehicle selector — a star rating with order count or a `Trusted by WRX and Focus RS owners` line. ~30px of vertical space on mobile.

Element

`(absent — proposed location: beneath the vehicle selector and above the FIND PARTS button)`

Observation

The above-fold viewport at scroll_y=0 contains: free-shipping announcement bar (`FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+`), AWDMods logo, hamburger menu, search bar, vehicle selector dropdowns, and FIND PARTS button. No aggregate review rating, no customer count, no media mention, and no satisfaction guarantee is visible. The free-shipping bar communicates a shipping policy but is not a credibility signal — it does not answer `Can I trust this merchant?` for a first-time visitor.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust signal directly within the hero section, beneath the vehicle selector and above the FIND PARTS button — for example, a one-line element showing `★★★★★ 4.8 · 3,200+ orders shipped` or `Trusted by WRX and Focus RS owners since [year]`. If aggregate review data is available from the Shopify store, a star rating aggregate with order count is the highest-credibility option and takes approximately 30px of vertical space.

Why this matters

For automotive parts — a considered purchase where fitment errors are costly and returns inconvenient — trust signals at the initial impression point are more important than in impulse categories. A first-time visitor encountering a dark, unfamiliar site with no credibility markers and a vehicle-configuration form as the primary UX has no signal that the merchant is reliable.

Evidence anchors

  • DOM · e22
  • Visual · section-3-mobile.jpg @ y=917 (mobile)
    Featured Collection cards at scroll_y~917-1400 show VelourTex Floor Mats card with product image, brand name, full product title, price 'FROM $135.99', and a 'Made to Order' badge. The only interactive element on the card is a small orange circular '+' quick-add overlay button (approximately 40x40px) that appears at the bottom-right corner of the product image. There is no labeled 'Add to Cart' or 'Choose Options' button visible in the card body at rest state.

Source

Hero Section Psychology
GOLDOpen primary source ↗
VC-04LOWHeadlines & Buttons
FIND PARTS CTA Button Visually Distinct on Dark Hero
The blue FIND PARTS button on the mobile hero stands out clearly against the dark background — it's the only filled, bounded interactive element in the lower hero, satisfying the contrast-over-color principle. Visitors who engage with the vehicle selector can find the conversion action without hunting.
What changes: No change needed. If the hero gets a redesign, make sure the FIND PARTS button keeps its current contrast advantage.

Element

FIND PARTS button (e14 area, y=0)

Observation

The FIND PARTS primary action button in the hero widget uses a blue solid fill that contrasts adequately against the dark hero background and the semi-transparent selector dropdown backgrounds. The button is the visually dominant interactive element within the vehicle selector widget, satisfying the contrast-over-color principle.

Recommendation

No change required for CTA button contrast in this element. If the overall hero receives a redesign per `visual-cta F-02` and `visual-cta F-05`, ensure the FIND PARTS button retains its current contrast advantage and is not overridden by added headline or trust signal elements.

Why this matters

Maintaining CTA visual dominance within the vehicle selector widget ensures that visitors who engage with the selector can identify and tap the conversion action without hunting for it.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-1-mobile.jpg @ y=0 (mobile)
    Above-fold viewport at scroll_y=0 contains: free-shipping announcement bar ('FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+'), AWDMods logo, hamburger menu, search bar, vehicle selector dropdowns, and FIND PARTS button. No aggregate review rating, no customer count, no media mention, and no satisfaction guarantee is visible in this viewport.

Source

Color Psychology
SILVEROpen primary source ↗
VC-05MEDIUMHeadlines & Buttons
No Risk-Reversal Microcopy Adjacent to Add to Cart
The desktop newsletter copy names the specific vehicles AWDMods serves (`Focus RS, Focus ST, WRX, STI`) rather than using generic `stay updated` language. This specificity sets clear subscriber expectations and qualifies subscribers at signup — a Focus RS owner reads the line and immediately knows the value.
What changes: No change needed. Update the copy when new vehicle models are added to the catalog so the audience-naming stays current.

Element

(absent)

Observation

The Add to Cart zone on this page — quantity selector, ATC button, Add to Wishlist — contains no trust-reinforcing text. The free-shipping qualifier exists only in the footer at scroll_y 6972, roughly 6000px below the CTA. The star rating displayed above the price shows (0 reviews), which provides no social validation. At the moment a shopper taps Add to Cart, there is no on-screen signal about shipping cost, returns, or any form of purchase reassurance.

Recommendation

Insert one line of microcopy immediately below the Add to Cart button: 'Free shipping on most U.S. orders · Easy returns' (using the asterisked language from the footer if accuracy requires it). Keep it to a single row at 12px so it does not push the Wishlist link off-screen. If a specific return window or guarantee exists, leading with that is stronger: '30-day returns · Ships from Moorpark, CA.'

Why this matters

Baymard Institute usability research identifies the final tap as the highest-abandonment point in a mobile purchase flow. Risk-reversal statements placed at button proximity address the last hesitation at the exact moment the shopper is deciding whether to commit. The same statement buried in the footer is functionally invisible to the majority of shoppers who decide in the product zone.

Evidence anchors

  • Visual · section-2-mobile.jpg @ y=820 (mobile)
    The row below the Add to Cart button shows only a heart icon and Add to Wishlist text. No shipping assurance, return policy, or satisfaction guarantee appears in the ATC zone. The footer DOM slice confirms free shipping text is buried at scroll_y 6972: '* Free Shipping Applies to Most Contiguous U.S. Orders - Some Items May Not Qualify'.
  • Visual · section-1.jpg @ y=0 (desktop)
    The buy box in section-1.jpg shows the product title, price ($69.95), PayPal Pay Later widget area, and Add to Cart button. No trust-reinforcing text appears in the buy box zone adjacent to the button. The free shipping asterisk disclaimer and return policy are confirmed only in the footer DOM at scroll_y 1385 — over 1000px below the Add to Cart button position.
